Q1 — Place: There's this massive, blasted-out rock face on the side of a highway I used to drive down as a kid. Most people probably just saw a gray wall, but I remember staring at the alternating stripes of rust-red and pale gray running through it. I loved how the layers tilted at a sharp angle, as if the whole ground had been violently shoved upward eons ago. I still think about running my hands over the jagged edges where the dynamite had split it. It felt like looking at a history book where the pages were millions of years thick.
Q2 — Habit: Whenever I go for a walk, my pockets end up completely weighed down. I can't walk past an interesting pebble or a jagged fragment on the trail without picking it up. I'll constantly scrape them against each other to see which one leaves a powder, or hold them up to the light to check how they fracture. My friends think I'm just a hoarder of random gravel, but I just like holding them. To me, every single piece feels like a tiny, heavy puzzle holding clues about where it came from.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll pick how a crack forms in a concrete sidewalk. Most people just see broken pavement, but I see a mini tectonic event. Water seeps into a tiny fissure, and when it freezes, it expands. It's exactly like ice wedging on a mountain peak, slowly prying the solid mass apart over time. Then, tree roots push up from below, applying immense, slow-motion pressure until the surface finally buckles and faults. It's basically continental drift and glacial erosion happening right in the middle of a city street.
Q4 — Conflict: I got into a huge argument on a road trip because I made us pull over for an hour at what my friend called 'just a boring, dry ditch.' She was annoyed I was wasting time staring at a canyon wall when we had a schedule to keep. But the real story was that I was entirely captivated by the layers of sediment. I could see exactly where an ancient, massive flood had carved through the bedrock. I was so mesmerized by the deep time recorded in the walls that her impatience felt almost disrespectful to the landscape.
Q5 — Sound: The sound of waves pulling back over a beach made entirely of smooth, rounded stones. I first heard it on a trip to the coast when I was ten. It's this incredibly loud, hollow, rhythmic clattering as thousands of heavy rocks are dragged against each other by the undertow. It sticks with me because it's the sound of literal erosion happening in real-time. You are standing there listening to the ocean aggressively grind mountains down into sand. It's chaotic, yet perfectly inevitable.
Q6 — Body: I was at a flea market, and someone handed me what they claimed was a solid carving of natural turquoise. The second it dropped into my palm, my hand tried to hand it back before I even consciously looked at it. My body immediately registered that the weight-to-volume ratio was completely wrong, and the temperature was too warm to be genuine stone. My brain was still trying to admire the bright blue color, but my hands already knew it was just cheap, dyed resin pretending to be a mineral.
Q7 — Unworded: It's the feeling of holding a piece of the world that is millions of years old, and realizing how incredibly fleeting human life actually is. When I press my palm against a cold, solid outcrop of rock, I get this overwhelming sensation that the stone is a silent witness to eras of the earth I can't even fathom. It's a dizzying mix of feeling completely insignificant, yet deeply connected to the monumental, slow-motion engine of the planet.
